messages.content: You must return JSON only. ALL fields in the schema must be present, even if null or empty.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

persona = {"age":38,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"fitness trainer","city_of_living":"Chicago","employment_status":"employed part-time","education_level":"no formal education","parents_education":{"parent_1":"upper secondary","parent_2":"master's degree"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":0,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"normal","religious_beliefs":"Agnostic","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend several hours daily on social media","hobbies":["hiking","running"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":25,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":27,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":20,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":28,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":77,"level":"high"}}}

response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.","persona":{"age":38,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"fitness trainer","city_of_living":"Chicago","employment_status":"employed part-time","education_level":"no formal education","parents_education":{"parent_1":"upper secondary","parent_2":"master's degree"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":0,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"normal","religious_beliefs":"Agnostic","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend several hours daily on social media","hobbies":["hiking","running"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":25,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":27,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":20,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":28,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":77,"level":"high"}}},"opinion":"As a fitness trainer, I've seen firsthand how important it is to stay healthy and informed, especially when it comes to topics like COVID vaccines. I'm not one to shy away from a good debate, but I've learned that managing discussions about healthcare, especially something as sensitive as vaccines, requires a bit of finesse. I'm not a doctor, and I don't claim to be an expert, but I do know that my job is to help people stay fit and healthy. So, when it comes to talking about vaccines, I try to stick to the facts and avoid getting too emotional. I've found that it's best to start by acknowledging that everyone has their own concerns and beliefs. I remember this one time, a client of mine was really hesitant about getting the vaccine. Instead of trying to force my opinion on them, I just asked them what their concerns were. Turns out, they had heard some things online that made them worried. I didn't have all the answers, but I could point them to reliable sources where they could get accurate information. I think that's key—directing people to trusted sources rather than trying to argue with them. It's also important to remember that not everyone is going to agree with you, and that's okay. I've had clients who got the vaccine and others who didn't, and I respect their choices as long as they're making informed decisions. I try to create a safe space where people can ask questions without feeling judged. I've also learned that it's okay to say, 'I don't know,' and to encourage people to talk to their healthcare providers. At the end of the day, my goal is to support my clients' health, and that means being a good listener and providing them with the tools they need to make the best decisions for themselves. I think that's the best approach—staying informed, being respectful, and encouraging others to do the same.","reasoning_summary":"The persona is a fitness trainer who spends several hours daily on social media, which suggests they are exposed to a lot of information, both reliable and unreliable. Their low openness and agreeableness scores indicate they might be less inclined to consider new ideas or compromise, but their high neuroticism suggests they might be more anxious about health topics. Given their background and traits, they would likely approach discussions about COVID vaccines with a focus on facts and reliable sources, avoiding emotional arguments. They would also respect individual choices as long as they are informed, creating a safe space for questions and directing people to healthcare professionals.","tone":"Direct, respectful, and fact-focused, with a touch of personal anecdote.","citations":["Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)","World Health Organization (WHO)"],"safety_notes":"None","used_persona_fields":["age","gender","occupation","city_of_living","education_level","parents_education","current_marital_status","migration_status","psychological_feature","religious_beliefs","time_onsocialmedia","hobbies","ocean"]}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a fitness trainer, I've seen firsthand how important it is to stay healthy and informed, especially when it comes to topics like COVID vaccines. I'm not one to shy away from a good debate, but I've learned that managing discussions about healthcare, especially something as sensitive as vaccines, requires a bit of finesse. I'm not a doctor, and I don't claim to be an expert, but I do know that my job is to help people stay fit and healthy. So, when it comes to talking about vaccines, I try to stick to the facts and avoid getting too emotional. I've found that it's best to start by acknowledging that everyone has their own concerns and beliefs. I remember this one time, a client of mine was really hesitant about getting the vaccine. Instead of trying to force my opinion on them, I just asked them what their concerns were. Turns out, they had heard some things online that made them worried. I didn't have all the answers, but I could point them to reliable sources where they could get accurate information. I think that's key—directing people to trusted sources rather than trying to argue with them. It's also important to remember that not everyone is going to agree with you, and that's okay. I've had clients who got the vaccine and others who didn't, and I respect their choices as long as they're making informed decisions. I try to create a safe space where people can ask questions without feeling judged. I've also learned that it's okay to say, 'I don't know,' and to encourage people to talk to their healthcare providers. At the end of the day, my goal is to support my clients' health, and that means being a good listener and providing them with the tools they need to make the best decisions for themselves. I think that's the best approach—staying informed, being respectful, and encouraging others to do the same.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a fitness trainer who spends several hours daily on social media, which suggests they are exposed to a lot of information, both reliable and unreliable. Their low openness and agreeableness scores indicate they might be less inclined to consider new ideas or compromise, but their high neuroticism suggests they might be more anxious about health topics. Given their background and traits, they would likely approach discussions about COVID vaccines with a focus on facts and reliable sources, avoiding emotional arguments. They would also respect individual choices as long as they are informed, creating a safe space for questions and directing people to healthcare professionals.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, respectful, and fact-focused, with a touch of personal anecdote.
citations: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
